title: Tragacantha angustissima
intro:
There is limited information about Tragacantha angustissima. However, I can provide general information about a similar plant, Tragacanth (also known as Tragacanth gum or Tragacanth mucilage). 

Tragacanth is a type of plant-based adhesive derived from the sap of the Astragalus plant, specifically the Tragacanth gum-producing species of Astragalus. This plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its health benefits, including soothing digestive issues, reducing inflammation, and supporting wound healing. The bioactive constituents of Tragacanth include mucilage, fiber, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Tragacanth include topical creams, ointments, and poultices for wound care, as well as oral supplements and teas for digestive support.

side_effects: Tragacantha angustissima, also known as needle bush or needle broom, is a plant native to Africa. As with any plant, Tragacantha angustissima may cause allergic reactions in some individuals due to the presence of allergenic particles in the pollen and sap. Ingesting any part of the plant can be toxic and may lead to gastrointestinal issues, nausea, and vomiting. Tragacantha angustissima may also cause skin irritation and contact dermatitis in people who come into contact with the plant's thorns or sap. It is essential to handle the plant with caution and wear protective gear when handling it to minimize potential risks.
